site_pages.title: Adult Education
site_pages.full_text:
Adult Education
Build your skills to enter college, training programs or the workforce through free programs offered to residents in Flathead, Lincoln, Lake and Sanders counties. Both in-person and online services are available to anyone ages 16 and up. We're here to help you achieve your education and career goals.
Contact us at
AdultEd@fvcc.edu
or 406.756.3884 to get started.
English Language Learning
English Language Learning (ELL) classes teach adult, non-native speakers of English to communicate through speaking, reading and writing.
High School Equivalency
High School Equivalency Test prep classes are available to community members ages 16 and up.
FAQs
Anyone 16 and up can participate in our programs.
Individuals 16-18 years old must submit
high school or homeschool withdrawal letters, as well as other forms depending on age. Contact us for more details.
All of FVCC Adult Education services are free to Montana residents 16+. We require a pre-assessment for our programs to determine service needs. FVCC offers a scholarship for students wanting to obtain their high school equivalency tests.
Contact us to get scheduled for an intake and pre-assessment. Students 16 years old must meet with the director and a parent/guardian to go over required documentation prior to orientation.
HSE Prep
Distance learning opportunities are open year-round.
FVCC Kalispell prep classes begin every six weeks and run for five-week cohorts. Classes are Monday through Thursday, 9am-12:30pm. Study hall services are available Mondays after class.
LCC Libby prep classes are available Tuesdays and Thursdays in the morning and evening.
ELL classes are open enrollment starting in July through the end of March. Pre-assessments determine class placement, and online options are also available.
If you are looking for a copy of your High School Equivalency transcript or credential, go to DiplomaSender.com and create an account. You can also contact DiplomaSender directly by starting a chat at DiplomaSender.com or calling 1-855-313-5799.
Find more information here.
Connect with us at
AdultEd@fvcc.edu
or 406.756.3884
Contact Us

site_pages.title: English Language Learning
site_pages.full_text:
English Language Learning
English Language Learning (ELL) classes teach adult, non-native speakers of English to communicate through speaking, reading, and writing. The courses also provide cultural education for living and working in the United States.
Through interactive and engaging activities, students build their English language vocabulary to better integrate into local communities. Students are able to engage more effectively in government, education and workplace environments. The course also provides preparation for the U.S. Citizenship test.
Classes are open to enrollment starting in July and ending in March. Students will complete a pre-assessment to determine class placement, and online options are also available.
For class schedules please email
adulted@fvcc.edu
or call 406.756.3884.
Contact Us

site_pages.title: High School Equivalency
site_pages.full_text:
High School Equivalency
Congratulations on taking the next step forward in your career and education by completing your High School Equivalency Test!
FVCC offers both HiSET and GED testing for students to earn a high school equivalency diploma from the Montana Office of Public Instruction.
Montana residents 19 years or older may register for tests at
hiset.org
or
ged.com
. Montana residents age 16 to 18 must contact us for paperwork requirements prior to registering for testing.
HSE Preparation
The Adult Education Program offers prep courses year-round to help build the skills needed to be successful on all HiSET and GED subject tests: reading, writing, science, social studies, and math.
The class covers academic skills as well as career exploration and goal setting in order to support students moving from HSE completion into college and the workforce. Classes are offered in Kalispell and Libby, with testing support options available for students in Lake or Sanders counties.
To be eligible to take the HiSET or GED, students must be 16 years of age or older, complete paperwork requirements and have officially withdrawn from high school. Pre-registration for class is required.
How to Start
Email
adulted@fvcc.edu
or call 406.756.3884 for information, paperwork requirements and to register.
Attend orientation and take the Test of Adult Basic Education (TABE).
Start studying!
16yo checklist for FVCC
(PDF)
16yo checklist for LCC
(PDF)
17+ years old checklist
(PDF)
High School Withdrawal Letter
(PDF)
Homeschool Withdrawal Letter Template
(PDF)
The Test of Adult Basic Education is a diagnostic test used to determine an individual’s skill level and aptitudes. We test the areas of math and reading. Many students use the scores from this assessment to determine areas they are ready to test in on HSE tests.
New student orientation covers required paperwork, program options and expectations, HSE testing information and account setup, online homework program signup, services offered, scholarship information and TABE pre-testing. Once we have TABE pre-assessment scores, we will help create an individualized learning plan. Distance learning options are also available.
FVCC Class Schedule
5 week classes: Monday through Thursday, 9am-12:30pm
Study Hall: Monday through Thursday after class
LCC Class Schedule
Morning Classes: Tuesdays and Thursdays, 8:30am-11:30am
Evening Classes: Tuesdays and Thursdays, 5:30pm-8:30pm
We have many students whose lives are busy or students who live in rural areas, and they cannot attend classes on campus during the times offered. We provide both in-person and distance learning opportunities and will work with you to prepare for tests at times which fit your schedule. Contact us to discuss your options.
Students 16 years of age must participate in our classes. Students 17 or 18 must submit proper documentation to our program before being cleared for HSE testing on our campuses. Anyone 17 and up has the option to take the HSE tests on their own, and can also take the TABE pretest to determine if services are needed.
The HiSET tests students’ knowledge in math, reading, science, social studies and writing. Students must obtain a combined score of 45 to pass the HiSET.
The GED tests students’ knowledge in language arts, math, science, and social studies. Students must obtain a score of 145 on each test to pass.
When you are ready to start testing, visit
hiset.org
or
ged.com
to create an account, register and pay for testing. Your name and date of birth on your account must match your government-issued photo ID.
Check your account for testing locations and available dates. All tests are computer-based. In-person testing is available at FVCC and Libby campuses.
On testing day, make sure to bring your valid government-issued photo ID and $5 per test receipt from the Business Office to the testing center.
For any other questions regarding test sign up, please contact us at
adulted@fvcc.edu
.
GED: Computer-based tests are $26 each for 4 tests totaling $104. GED fees are paid through your GED account when testing is scheduled.
HiSET: Computer-based tests are $15 each for 5 tests totaling $75. HiSET fees are paid through your HiSET account when testing is scheduled.
An additional $5 per test is due to the Business Office on testing day for both HiSET and GED.

site_pages.title: Workforce Training & Professional Workshops
site_pages.full_text:
Workforce Training & Professional Workshops
The FVCC Continuing Education Center and Workforce Training Program provides high-quality, relevant training opportunities that address the needs of local employers. Through local economic, community and workforce partnerships, training programs are consistently adapted to meet business and professional needs.
Through the Continuing Education Center and Workforce Training Program, professionals have access to high-quality and affordable professional development and personal growth courses.
Businesses may also coordinate organization-specific training workshops addressing custom development needs by working closely with the Continuing Education Center staff.
Workforce Training
Learn more about
workforce training
at FVCC.
Apprenticeship Opportunities
There are many credit and non-credit programs at FVCC that can provide the specific related training instruction (RTI) for an apprenticeship for your business or organization. Learn more about
apprenticeship opportunities
at FVCC.
Small Business Development Center
The
Kalispell Regional Small Business Development Center (SBDC)
can assist you with business counseling, marketing, financial projections, startup assistance and more – free of charge!
The Small Business Development Center can assist with many issues facing small-business owners, including:
Start a new business – we can help you turn your business idea into reality.
Business finance assistance – we'll work with you to understand financials and increase your profits.
Business Plans – the SBDC provides assistance writing your business plans.

Credentialing and Re-Certification
FVCC Continuing Education offers business and computer workshops that are eligible for CEUs through the Continuing Education Center. Many classes also qualify for Teacher Renewal Units (TRUs) and Nursing continuing education contact hours.
The Continuing Education Center can assist with developing or improving your current specialized certification training, including CEU, CPE, CLE and Teacher Renewal Units at your selected site.
The FVCC team will approve the course content and the instructor, register participants, provide a roster, award contact hours, maintain permanent records and print participant transcripts.
Contact the FVCC Continuing Education Center for more information: (406) 756-3832 or email

site_pages.title: Customized Team Training
site_pages.full_text:
Customized Team Training
Teams that learn together work better and more effectively for your organization. Let FVCC help you create the team training that you need to succeed.
FVCC’s customized team training allows your business or organization to achieve your goals and benefit through increased operational efficiency, improved employee skills and morale, and a shared common mission.
Consider how they can support the goals you have for your business or organization!
If you would like to create training that will meet your specific, unique training objectives, FVCC Continuing Education offers Customized Training options.
Here are some success stories:
A local non-profit wanted to elevate the level of customer service in the organization. We customized an existing 3-hour program for their front-line staff and created a new 3-hour program for their supervisory staff.
A local bank wanted to create a career development program for future potential leaders in their business. We paired a variety of industry trainers and faculty with administrative team leaders from the bank to create a 9-month series.
A local timber company needed their operators to achieve a higher level in their licensing and knew other businesses in the area would also benefit from such training. We created the curriculum they needed, had it approved by the State, and led every student to pass their licensing exam.
We’d love to match your business training objectives with a skilled instructor or industry trainer.
View the FVCC Team Training brochure
.
Please call or email Jodi Smith, Workforce Training Director 406.756.3833 or email
jsmith@fvcc.edu
.

site_pages.full_text:
Meeting Facilitation
Invite a skilled facilitator to help you prepare and guide your meeting or retreat agenda to support topics from strategic planning to difficult discussion topics to brainstorming innovative change.
A facilitator objectively oversees the meeting schedule, neutrally raises important questions and allows participation by all team or committee members.
Meeting facilitation includes three hours of meeting time to develop an agenda, prepare for the meeting and write a meeting summary.
3 hours | $500
Additional time will be invoiced at $100/hour facilitation time; $50/hr preparation time
Please call or email Jodi Smith, Workforce Training Director 406.756.3833 or email
jsmith@fvcc.edu
.
About the meeting facilitator:
Jodi Smith earned her Facilitation Skills Certificate through the Association for Talent Development (ATD).

site_pages.title: Conference & Retreat Planning
site_pages.full_text:
Conference & Retreat Planning
Considering hosting a conference or large event?
The FVCC Continuing Education Center can assist with conference registration. You can expect quality assistance to coordinate registration and related conference and retreat planning services for you.
Basic registration services include:
Registering participants in our software system
Processing registration payments including checks, and credit cards
Printing enrollment rosters
Compiling accounting summary of funds.
Intermediate registration services include the basic services plus:
Preparing name badges
Maintaining course and instructor information
Awarding Continuing Education Units or other pre-approved certification requirements
Assisting with check-in at conference
Advanced registration services include the basic and intermediate services plus:
Copying handout materials at cost
Inserting handout materials into folders
Additional services such as catering referrals, media equipment, or facility information may be available upon request.
Contact the Continuing Education Center at (406) 756-3832 or email at
ceinfo@fvcc.edu
for fee schedule and additional information.

site_pages.title: Continuing Education
site_pages.full_text:
Continuing Education
Discover a passion. Sharpen a skill. Enrich your life. Continue your lifelong journey of learning with FVCC Continuing Education.
Summer 2026 Catalog
View Summer 2026 Catalog
(PDF)
View and Register for Classes
Current Instructors Portal
Courses are offered on a spring, fall and summer timeline with different classes available each season. Space is limited so register early to ensure your spot.
Payment is required at the time of registration.
Adults age 65 or older may receive a $5 discount on tuition fees for non-credit classes that cost $30 or more. Special programs may not qualify for this discount.
Reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities can be arranged by contacting the Continuing Education Center.
Unless otherwise noted, all classes are held on the FVCC campus. Check your receipt and email reminder for class location.
Classes that do not receive sufficient enrollment may be canceled.
If a class is canceled, you will be notified and a full refund will be issued.
Cancelations received 14 calendar days prior to the start of a class or workshop will receive a full refund. Students must contact Continuing Education at 406.756.3832 or
ceinfo@fvcc.edu
to cancel and request the full refund.
No refund is issued for cancellations received less than 14 calendar days prior to the start of the class or workshop.
Continuing Education classes at Flathead Valley Community College provide intriguing and engaging opportunities for life-long learners. We offer a variety of personal development, business and workforce training, and general interest classes and activities.
We publish three catalogs each year. The Continuing Education Curriculum Committee reviews new course proposals on an on-going basis. We welcome applications from potential instructors and professionals with a wide range of educational and life experiences who demonstrate subject matter expertise and teaching capability.
Course Proposal Deadlines:
Fall Semester (Sept-Dec):  May 15
Spring Semester (Jan-May): October 1
Summer Semester (May-Aug): February 1
Please complete the
Instructor Interest Form
and a member of our team will reach out with the next steps.
Workforce Development
High-quality and affordable non-credit training programs.
Ed-2-Go Online Non-Credit Courses
Learn from your home with over 250 online classes offered through our partner Ed-2-Go.
Free Community Lectures
Fascinating topics of discussion for the broader Flathead and Lincoln Counties.
Kids College
Week-long classes and camps during the summer for kids ages six and older
Small Business Development Center
(SBDC) assists with business counseling, marketing, financial projections, startup assistance and more–free of charge!
Campus Farm
FVCC’s working educational farm focused on regenerative vegetable and fruit production, offering courses throughout the year.
Contact Continuing Education at
ceinfo@fvcc.edu
or 406.756.3832
Email Us

site_pages.title: Kids College
site_pages.full_text:
Kids College
Spend your summer discovering new worlds at Flathead Valley Community College with fun, hands-on activities and extraordinary teachers at FVCC Kids College.
FVCC’s Kids College is a summer program for students ages 6-18. Kids College consists of week-long classes designed to stimulate creative minds, build healthy bodies and challenge adventurous souls. With subjects in art, science and technology, language and culture, and computer game design, there is something for every young learner!
Summer 2026 Registration
View Summer 2026 Catalog
(PDF)
View and Register for Classes
Kids College will run the weeks of:
June 15-19
June 22-26
July 6-10
July 13-17
July 20-24
Important Reminders
Parents or a designated adult must sign the child in/out each class day.
Please arrive no earlier than 5 minutes prior to the start of class.
Plan to pick up your child/children immediately at the end of class. If you will be delayed, notify the Continuing Education Center at 406.756.3832. Your child will be brought to the office for you to pick up.
Kids College is not an all-day camp. Your child/children are NOT supervised on campus during the lunch hour.
Students are expected to behave in a non-disruptive manner during and after class and may be dismissed from a class for behavior violations.
Only registered students are allowed in class.
Students are responsible for their own belongings. If items are left behind, they will be taken to the Continuing Education Center office.
Students may bring water and a snack and should wear appropriate clothing including closed-toe shoes or sandals.
Cell phones must be turned off during class.
Find information on
cancellation and refund policy
and other Continuing Education policies and procedures.
Visit Us
FVCC Continuing Education
215 Arts & Technology Building
Office Hours: 8 AM-5 PM, Monday through Friday
Email us here or call 406.756.3832 for more information.
Email Us

site_pages.title: Senior Institute
site_pages.full_text:
Senior Institute
The Senior Institute at FVCC is for community members 65+. The Senior Institute is a vibrant community of older adults who are excited to learn, build relationships, and explore new ideas. From long-time returning students to those new to FVCC, the Senior Institute is a welcoming community for anyone who is interested in learning new skills and growing their social network. Join us in the upcoming Senior Institute to see what the excitement is about!
Senior Citizen Discount for FVCC Credit Courses
Adults 65 years of age and up who are residents of Flathead and Lincoln counties receive discounted tuition and fees when registering for credit courses. See the current
Tuition and Fees Schedule
for more information.
Spring 2026 Edition
Fall 2026 Edition
Fridays, February 13-March 20, 2026
Classes 1-3:05pm
Program and refreshments 3:30-4pm
In-person registration and registration processing begins January 28 at 8am.
See the
Spring 2026 class schedule
(PDF). Enrollment is limited.
Check back here this summer for upcoming Fall 2026 dates.

Student Government & Leadership

site_pages.title: Student Government & Leadership
site_pages.full_text:
Student Government & Leadership
Do you enjoy helping others, have fresh, creative ideas and want to gain leadership skills while making a positive impact on your fellow students’ college experience? The following opportunities might be for you.
Student Government
Student Government advocates in the interest of all students, provides leadership opportunities and enhances the college experience by unifying and empowering its members.
The group is fiscally responsible for student activity fees and welcomes any questions or concerns regarding allocation of appropriate resources.
To get involved or get more information, connect with
studentgovernment@fvcc.edu
.
Student Leadership Programs
FVCC’s Student Leadership Program supports student leaders by building leadership skills, awareness of leadership styles and theories, and encourages personal growth through a variety of experiences and practice.
All students are welcome to participate at no cost. If you currently hold a leadership position or are interested in becoming a leader of a campus organization, this program is designed for you.
The kickoff to our Student Leadership Program begins with a two-day retreat in the fall with transportation, lodging, food and materials provided at no cost to current students.
Leadership workshops are provided throughout the year to further develop leadership skills and challenge you to apply the skills you've learned to real-world situations. The program ends with a celebratory awards ceremony in May.
Connect with the Student Engagement Office at
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
or 406.756.3908 for more information.

site_pages.title: Student Organizations
site_pages.full_text:
Student Organizations
FVCC offers a wide variety of student organizations that promote and enhance student success. We encourage you to explore and enjoy exciting new experiences, build lifelong friendships and make every moment of your college experience memorable.
Art Club
The Art Club is committed to furthering education and inspiration for developing artists and the community.
Advisor
: David Regan | 406.756.3993 |
dregan@fvcc.edu
Chess & Games Club
The Chess Club provides a safe, inclusive and fun environment and welcomes all students regardless of skill level.
Advisor
: Russ Lamson |
rlamson@fvcc.edu
Disc Golf Club
The Disc Golf Club is dedicated to growing the sport on campus by creating a fun, inclusive, and supportive community for players of all skill levels while organizing both recreational and competitive events that promote good sportsmanship.
Advisor
: Ranell Mueller |
rmueller@fvcc.edu
Firearms Enthusiasts Guild
Firearms Enthusiasts Guild is open to all students interested in improving their knowledge of firearms safe handling, technical expertise and historical significance. The group also engages in activities to promote the Firearms Technology program.
Advisor
: Michael Garcia |
mgarcia@fvcc.edu
First Gen Club
Our mission is to foster connections and a sense of belonging for first-generation students.
Advisor
: Jennifer McKinney |
jmckinney@fvcc.edu
Genders & Sexualities Alliance
The GSA is an inclusive group that provides a social support, serves the community and spreads awareness.
Advisor
: Beth Romain | 406.756.3923 |
eromain@fvcc.edu
Honors Club
Open to all FVCC students who are interested in or currently enrolled in the Honors Program.
Advisor
: Marty Mullins |
mmullins@fvcc.edu
Intervarsity Christian Fellowship
A campus ministry that provides students the opportunity to be part of a small group bible study, outreach events, strong loving community and missional activities. The club invites students into caring, diverse communities where they can build friendships and explore the Christian faith.
Advisor
: Janice Heil | 406.756.3373 |
jheil@fvcc.edu
Mask and Hammer (Theatre Club)
The theatre club helps provide support to and promote all theatrical events and theatre students at FVCC. The club seeks to engage students in all aspects of theatre including acting, set design, lights, sound and production.
Advisor
: Rich Haptonstall |
rhaptonstall@fvcc.edu
Music Club
The music club is committed to bringing the joy of music to the students of FVCC.
Advisor
: Nicole Sanford | 406.756.4813 |
nsanford@fvcc.edu
Native American Student Association
The Native American Student Association supports the academic and personal success of American Indian/Alaskan Native students and all FVCC students with an emphasis on promoting cultural awareness and creating a welcoming campus.

site_pages.title: Phi Theta Kappa
site_pages.full_text:
Phi Theta Kappa
Phi Theta Kappa (PTK) is an international honor society for two-year colleges whose mission is to recognize and encourage scholarship among college students. Since 1918, this prestigious organization has provided professional and leadership development, scholarship access and engagement opportunities for more than 3 million members worldwide.
Membership
Students who achieve outstanding academic achievement are eligible for membership. Criteria include the completion of 12 semester credits and a minimum grade point average of 3.4.
FVCC’s local chapter, Alpha Iota Pi, provides members with opportunities for individual growth and development through participation in the four hallmarks of Phi Theta Kappa: scholarship, leadership, fellowship and service.
Benefits
Scholarship
: Because membership demonstrates a commitment to scholarship, PTK provides more than $37 million in scholarships to four-year colleges and universities to support transfer students.
Leadership
: PTK members have many opportunities to develop leadership skills, such as serving in an officer position or chairing a committee.
Fellowship
: Members are provided several opportunities to participate in social activities throughout the school year.
Service
: Phi Theta Kappa is a dedicated service organization. Members participate in activities designed to improve their colleges and their local communities.
Commencement
: Each Phi Theta Kappan is entitled to wear a distinguished gold and navy blue stole and tassel at commencement. These items can be purchased or earned through active participation in your chapter.
For more information, contact:
Wendy Jeschke
Program Director, Student Engagement
Molly Maxwell
Associate Professor, Math

site_pages.full_text:
Community Involvement
The Student Engagement Office works to connect you to the community through educational and volunteer opportunities. Check out ways to get involved on campus and in the community.
Service Learning
Get class credit for volunteering.
Volunteer
Give back to the community.

Saturday volunteer events.
Community-Based Work Study Program
Off-campus work-study opportunities

Information on using the Food Pantry, donating, and getting involved.
AmeriCorps
Information on AmeriCorps opportunities.
Civic Engagement & Voter Registration
Information and resources to become civically engaged and registered to vote.
Students
To participate in service learning, volunteering, or Service Saturdays, complete the Community Involvement Agreement and Liability form and send it to the student engagement office (Blake Hall 155) or email to student-engagement@fvcc.edu.

site_pages.title: Service Learning & Volunteer Programs
site_pages.full_text:
Service Learning & Volunteer Programs
Service learning is an educational opportunity for students, faculty, and community partners to work together to enhance student learning while meeting the needs of the community. Through meaningful service activities and structured reflection, students gain valuable hands-on experience, leadership skills, and a sense of lifelong civic responsibility.
Students serve 15 hours at a nonprofit organization, school, or government entity directly related to the learning outcomes of their course. Upon completion, students receive a service learning indicator on their official transcript and appropriate credit for their class.
Service Learning & Volunteer Opportunities
Some courses incorporate service learning as part of their curriculum. Besides influencing your grade, it also gives you hands-on experience, opportunities to explore career fields, and upon successful completion of hours a service learning indicator on your official transcript.
Student Agreement and Liability Form
Complete and submit the
Volunteer Agreement and Liability Form
to the student engagement office (Blake Hall 155) or email at
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
.
MobileServe Account
Once you submit the form above, you will receive an email to complete an account for MobileServe. You will need your student ID number to finish creating the account. MobileServe will be used to track and verify your volunteer hours.
Find a Community Partner
Each course that participates in service learning has specific learning outcomes that relate to the class. You are required to serve for 15 hours at a nonprofit, school or government entity that directly relates to learning outcomes found in the course syllabus. To find a community partner, log on to your MobileServe account, click on “Events” on the left dashboard, click on “Discover” and search locally for opportunities. Click on an opportunity you are interested in, select “I Want to Help” and reach out to the organization contact person to set up a time to serve. Having trouble finding a community partner? Ask your professor! They will most likely have a few in mind.
Track Your Hours
Log in to MobileServe and select “Log Your Hours.” An email will be sent to the organization supervisor to verify them.
Service Learning Indicator
Once the 15 hours are complete, submitted, and verified on MobileServe your professor will be able to view them and a Service Learning Indicator will be placed on your official transcript. This will show universities that you have made a difference in your community and have valuable hands-on experience that will help you be successful in your college career and beyond.
Service learning is a way to enhance your courses by using a hands-on teaching methodology, and it gives students the opportunity to fulfill unmet needs within the community. To participate in the service learning program, follow the steps below:
Faculty Interest Form
Request a faculty interest form by emailing service-learning@fvcc.edu and submit it to FVCC’s student engagement office (Blake Hall 155).
MobileServe Account
Once you submit the form above, you will receive an email to complete an account for MobileServe. With this account, you will be able to track your student’s hours and verify appropriate organizations. Organizations must be a nonprofit organization, school, or government entity that ties directly with the learning outcomes of your course.
Student Reflection
Incorporating a way for students to reflect on their service is a vital portion of service learning. This can be an essay, journal, discussion, presentation, etc.
Student Hours
MobileServe will be used to track student service learning hours. Once the student logs their hours on their MobileServe account, an email will be sent to the community partner to verify the hours.
Service learning is a great way to fulfill the unmet needs within your organization while giving students the opportunity to enhance their learning with hands-on experience. If you would like to be a Community Partner with FVCC’s Service Learning program, follow the steps below:
Community Partner Questionnaire
Submit a completed
Community Partner Questionnaire form
to wjeschke@fvcc.edu or turn it into the student engagement office (Blake Hall 155).
VolunteerMatch Account
Create a free account on
volunteermatch.org
Post Organization Needs
Post opportunities for students to sign up for. Without posting students will not be able to see your organization. For information on how to post opportunities for students, visit
vmhelp.zendesk.com
Connecting With Students
Students will see the opportunities on
MobileServe
, an online platform that allows verification and tracking of hours. When students sign up for opportunities on MobileServe you will be able to discuss times with them and fulfill your organization needs.
Tracking Hours
MobileServe will be used to track student volunteer hours. Once the student logs their hours on their MobileServe account, an email will be sent to you to verify their hours.

site_pages.title: Community-Based Work-Study
site_pages.full_text:
Community-Based Work-Study
The Community-Based Work Study program is designed to give students an opportunity to earn their financial aid awards (work-study funds) through employment at off-campus community service agencies such as local non-profit organizations, government entities and schools. The program provides students with the opportunity to make meaningful contributions to local organizations, learn about community needs and develop professional skills.
This program operates during the academic year only.
The Community-Based Work Study program allows you to earn work study funds through an off-campus non-profit organization. Students may earn $12–$14 per hour and can work between 8-19 hours per week depending upon award.
Eligibility
In order to be part of the program, you must qualify for state or federal work study funding and be able to work a minimum of 8 hours per week.
How to Apply
Check your work study eligibility. Students eligible for work study funding should receive an award letter from the FVCC Financial Aid Office prior to the start of the semester. You can also check your work study eligibility by contacting the financial aid office in LRC 117 or by calling 406-756-3850.
Finalize your class and availability schedule, accounting for any extra-curricular activities you may have.
Review and apply for student jobs at the
FVCC Human Resources Student Employment page
.
Next Steps
Someone from the student engagement office will contact you to schedule an interview.
After you have received and accepted an offer, you will be notified by the human resources office to complete the on-boarding process.
Important Things to Know
The amount of work study award that you have been awarded is the amount that you are eligible to earn during the school year. You cannot earn more money than your award. Any money not used by the end of the school year is lost and does not carry over into the next year. The program does not continue into the summer.
There are a limited number of spaces available to participate in this program. You need to work hard to secure a job in a timely fashion if you hope to participate.
You must work a minimum of 8 hours per week to participate in the CBWS program.
You may work up to 19 hours per week during the times that FVCC is in session. You do not have to work over semester or spring breaks, but you may choose to do so and are eligible to work 40 hours per week during these times if you are pre-registered for the following semester. Work with your site supervisor to determine your schedule during the breaks. You cannot continue your work into the summer months.
You are ultimately responsible for keeping count of the amount of money that you earn and ensuring that you do not work in excess of the amount of work study money you’ve been awarded. Determine in advance how you will spread your hours over the course of the year to align with your work study award. Figure your hours properly; your organization will depend upon you once you’ve started working for them.
You must commit to an organization for the full year.
If you have issues, you need to try to work them out with your supervisor at your site. You can contact the Student Engagement Coordinator for support, if needed. Should you choose to leave a job, you are expected to give at least two-week notice.
Please note that it is a privilege and not a right to participate in the CBWS program. Agency supervisors ultimately determine if they think you’d be a good match for the organization or not and also have the right to dismiss you for any reason (barring discrimination). The Student Engagement Coordinator also determines whether you’re an appropriate fit for work in the community in FVCCs’ name.
As this is a federal program, there is a very strict division between church and state. Regardless of the organization you are working for, you may not practice religious proselytizing, care for church property, or become involved in partisan politics. Your agencies are aware of this, and should not ask you to participate in such activities.
Your site supervisor will be asked to complete an evaluation of your work before the close of each semester.
We will also ask that you provide us with formal feedback about your experience working at your organization at the close of the spring semester.
Please feel free to come see Wendy Jeschke at any time regarding your Community Based Work Study experience. Wendy can be reached at (406) 756-3908 or at
wjeschke@fvcc.edu
or in LRC 102.
Flathead Valley Community College’s Community-Based Work Study program can assist your organization fulfill its mission.
How It Works
Students are available to work between 8-19 hours per week at your agency.
Eligibility
In order to be eligible with no financial responsibility, your organization must:
Be a federal, state or local public agency or a private nonprofit organization.
Employ students as reading tutors for preschool-aged children or elementary school children; or
Employ students as math tutors for children in elementary school through ninth grade; or
Employ students to perform family literacy activities in a family literacy project that provides services to families with preschool or elementary age children; or employ students in community service activities, and is performing civic education and participation activities in community service projects.
Identify a direct supervisor and provide supervision during scheduled work hours.
In order to be eligible with a 10% cost share, your organization must:
Be a federal, state or local public agency or a private nonprofit organization.
Sign a statement that FVCC does not own, operate, or control the organization or agency.
Provide a signed letter stating that the agency cannot afford to pay the regular non federal share (30%).
Identify a direct supervisor and provide supervision during scheduled work hours.
How To Apply
In order to be considered as a host organization in the Community-Based Work Study program, please complete the
Community Partner Questionnaire
and send it to Wendy Jeschke at wjeschke@fvcc.edu or (406) 756-3908.
After receiving your form, someone from the student engagement office will contact you.
Community Mentor Program
The community mentor program focuses on connecting FVCC students with members of the community that work in the student’s career field of interest. Students will be given a chance to learn what it is like working in their field of interest and to know what is required of them to enter that field. They will also be able to develop their professional network and become more engaged in the community.
The goal of the meetings is to help you grow and keep track of your professional progress in your field of interest. You should meet a minimum of four times throughout the course of the year in a work setting or public space. To participate follow the steps below:
Complete the
Community Mentor Student Application form
and submit it to the student engagement office (LRC 102) or scan and email to
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
.
Based on your submitted form we will match you with a mentor.
Meet for the first time and complete the
Community Program Goals
form together and sign the Community Mentor Program Expectations form.
Meet at least three more times throughout the year.
After the last meeting, complete the Student Evaluation form.
The goal of the meetings is to help your mentee progress in their field of interest. You should meet a minimum of four times throughout the course of the year in a work setting or public space. To participate follow the steps below:
Complete the
Mentor Application form
and submit to the student engagement office in the Learning Resource Center (LRC) room 102, or scan and email to
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
.
Based on your submitted form we will match you with a mentee.
Meet for the first time and complete the
Community Mentor Program Goals form
together and sign the
Expectations and Accountability form
.
Meet at least three more times throughout the year.
Complete the
Community Mentor Check-in form
after the second meeting.
After the last meeting, complete the Mentor Program Evaluation form.
If you have any questions please email
student-engagement@fvcc.edu
, call (406) 756-3908, or stop by the student engagement office at Flathead Valley Community College, Learning Resource Center (LRC) room 102.

site_pages.title: Voter Registration
site_pages.full_text:
Voter Registration
FVCC encourages students to participate in all local, statewide and federal elections to increase representation of student voices in government and foster lifelong habits of civic engagement.
Register, find info on candidates and issues, navigate voting laws, and turn out at the polls.
Mail in voter registration forms are located in the Student Center 102. More information and an online form can be found here:
Flathead County
Lincoln County
Not sure where you are registered to vote? Find out here.
Get Involved
Upcoming Events on Campus
September 17: Voter Registration Day & Constitution Day
October 7-11: Voter Education Week
October 29: Vote Early Day
November 5: Election Day
More Resources
Become civically engaged and volunteer to be an election judge
Learn more at Montana Election & Voter Services
To get involved or for more info, contact Wendy Jeschke at
wjeschke@fvcc.edu
or 406.756.3908

site_pages.title: Student Housing
site_pages.full_text:
Student Housing
Built in 2017, FVCC’s student housing in Founders Hall offers comfortable single and double bedroom units with full kitchens and bathrooms at competitive rental rates on campus. All units are equipped with secure entrances.
The living space also features coin-operated laundry facilities, private study and meeting areas, mailboxes, community spaces, bike racks and free parking. Safe walking paths allow residents easy access to the rest of campus, as well as to nearby stores and restaurants.
Apply for Housing
Room assignments are offered in order of application completion. All completed applications will be placed on a waitlist until guarantees/room assignments are made.

For applications to be considered complete and placed on the waitlist, the applicant must submit an application and pay the $300 application fee/security deposit to the FVCC Business Office. If we are unable to secure waitlisted applicants a spot or the application is canceled, this fee will be refunded. The application fee can be paid in three ways:
In Person
Business Services in Blake Hall, Room 132
By Mail
Residence Life, Flathead Valley Community College
777 Grandview Dr. Kalispell, MT 59901
By Phone
Call Business Services at 406-756-3831 to pay the application fee over the phone.
Resources and Forms
Residence Life Handbook
(PDF)
Cancellation Request Form
(Google form)
Special Request Form
(Google form)
Student Housing Packing List
(PDF)
Air Conditioning Rental Agreement
(Google form)
Intent to Vacate Form
(Google form)
Student Housing Checkout List
(PDF)
Founders Hall Resident Information Form
(Google form)
For student housing questions and information, please email
reslife@fvcc.edu
, call (406) 756-4586 or contact your student housing team.
Misty Koyuncu
Coordinator, Residence Life
Student Housing FAQs
Students will begin receiving notifications in May for Fall semester housing assignments, and in December for Spring semester housing assignments.
Single studio apartments are in high demand and go quickly when spaces open up. If we can place you in housing but no single studio apartments are available, we will offer you a spot in a two-bedroom shared apartment. You will have the opportunity to cancel your application if you are not satisfied with your housing assignment.
Financial aid may cover your housing costs depending on the amount of your overall financial aid package. If your net credit after paying your tuition and fees is sufficient to also cover your housing costs, then your aid can be applied to housing.
If your financial aid package covers your entire bill, including rent, then you do not need to come up with rent costs before disbursement. If this applies to you, please contact FVCC Business Services right away to inform them. You can find their desk in Blake Hall or call 406-756-3831.
Priority for student housing is given to students enrolled for a minimum of 12 credits per semester (except for Summer). Part-time students are eligible to live in the Residence Hall as space permits. Dropping to or below part-time status from full-time status does not automatically cancel a housing contract. If you need to drop to or below part-time status, you can complete the Special Request Form
.
We are not able to accommodate children or families.
If you are registered for at least 12 credits for the following Fall, you do not have to take classes to stay in student housing over Summer. If you are taking less than 12 credits or will not be a student in the following Fall, then you must take at least 3 credits to stay in student housing over Summer. Please keep in mind that if you rely on financial aid to pay for student housing costs, this aid may not be available to you during Summer and you will still be required to pay housing charges. Contact
finaidinfo@fvcc.edu
for more information and to inquire about the status of your financial aid.
No. FVCC is committed to providing an alcohol-free learning environment. Alcohol is prohibited in the Residence Hall, academic buildings, parking lots and all other areas of college property, regardless of your age.
No. Use, possession or distribution of illegal drugs and other controlled substances including medical or recreational marijuana or drug paraphernalia is prohibited on campus. Although Montana state law permits the use of medical marijuana, federal laws prohibit marijuana use, possession, or cultivation at educational institutions which receive federal funds. The use, possession, or cultivation of marijuana for medical or recreational purposes is therefore not allowed in any FVCC housing or any other FVCC property, nor is it allowed at any college-sponsored event or activity off campus.
No. The furniture provided by the college must remain in your room.
If you and your roommate are experiencing difficulty residing together, a Residence Life staff member will work with you and your roommate to learn to anticipate problems and develop coping strategies. If remediation strategies fail, a room change may be possible, depending on room availability. All room changes need to be approved by the Residence Life Coordinator; no room changes will be made before the seventh day of classes.
Rooms are reserved for specific students and are non-transferable. Founders Hall is a private residence for FVCC students, with access limited to residents, their guests, and authorized individuals. Residents are responsible for their room's activities, even if absent. Roommates must respect each other's privacy and agree on shared space usage. Cohabitation is prohibited. Guests are allowed if they respect the rights of other students and roommates. No more than ten people are allowed in a room at any time due to noise and fire hazards. Overnight guests require roommate permission, must follow all rules, and are limited to three nights per month, regardless of whether it is a different guest each stay. Non-resident guests must be escorted and follow all policies. Hosts are responsible for their guests' actions and may face disciplinary action for violations.
Yes, if you have not cancelled your housing commitment for the following semester.

sections.heading: Misty Koyuncu
sections.content: Coordinator, Residence Life

sections.heading: Student Housing FAQs
sections.content:
Students will begin receiving notifications in May for Fall semester housing assignments, and in December for Spring semester housing assignments.
Single studio apartments are in high demand and go quickly when spaces open up. If we can place you in housing but no single studio apartments are available, we will offer you a spot in a two-bedroom shared apartment. You will have the opportunity to cancel your application if you are not satisfied with your housing assignment.
Financial aid may cover your housing costs depending on the amount of your overall financial aid package. If your net credit after paying your tuition and fees is sufficient to also cover your housing costs, then your aid can be applied to housing.
If your financial aid package covers your entire bill, including rent, then you do not need to come up with rent costs before disbursement. If this applies to you, please contact FVCC Business Services right away to inform them. You can find their desk in Blake Hall or call 406-756-3831.
Priority for student housing is given to students enrolled for a minimum of 12 credits per semester (except for Summer). Part-time students are eligible to live in the Residence Hall as space permits. Dropping to or below part-time status from full-time status does not automatically cancel a housing contract. If you need to drop to or below part-time status, you can complete the Special Request Form
.
We are not able to accommodate children or families.
If you are registered for at least 12 credits for the following Fall, you do not have to take classes to stay in student housing over Summer. site_pages.title: Wachholz College Center
site_pages.full_text:
Wachholz College Center
The first venue of its kind in northwest Montana, the Paul D. Wachholz College Center expands access to educational and cultural opportunities for campus and the community. From musical performances spanning multiple genres to engaging speaker series, family-friendly shows, and dynamic dance productions, there is something for everyone.

Quote icon
Philanthropy and education have been lifelong  values for my family. I trust FVCC to help me  achieve my philanthropic goals and see the  impact I make on students’ lives. I am proud to  support FVCC and leave a legacy of education  for our community.
Suzy Williams
longtime donor and friend of FVCC
Quote icon
As a surgeon at Logan Health, I see the need for well-trained healthcare professionals in our community. By supporting the college and taking advantage of the Montana Endowment Tax Credit, I'm ensuring students have the tools they need to enter the workforce and make an impact.
Melissa Kaptanian, MD
Foundation Board of Directors
Quote icon
My father spent his life as a grading contractor, and I wanted to honor his legacy by helping students pursue careers in this field. By establishing the Walter Kuhn Sr. Memorial endowed scholarship, I'm supporting a lifetime of heavy equipment operators who will build the infrastructure that keeps our community strong.
Walter Kuhn, Jr.

site_pages.title: History & Mission
site_pages.full_text:
History & Mission
Flathead Valley Community College was established in 1967 as a result of collaborative efforts by local citizens supporting area students and employers by providing higher education without leaving the valley.
History
Local, affordable higher education in the Flathead Valley started as a dream of Owen Sowerwine, chairman of the local school board. In 1960, Sowerwine voiced concerns regarding the lack of post-secondary education opportunities for young people in the valley.
A study conducted in 1959 showed that only 20% of Flathead High School graduates were pursuing higher education. Owen and four other visionary community members – Bill McClaren, Thelma Hetland, Les Sterling and Norm Beyer – were determined to create a local higher education option for Flathead Valley students.
Owen traveled around the country to observe different models of higher education. By 1963, after studying several junior colleges, vocational colleges, four-year universities and community colleges, he concluded that a community college was the best fit for the Flathead Valley.
It was time for action. From 1964 to 1967, college advocates worked diligently to inform citizens about the purpose of a community college and what it could offer in the way of vocational education, college transfer preparation and more.
On April 1, 1967, thanks to the generous support of the citizens, a ballot election passed by more than 1,000 votes and Flathead Valley Community College was established.
Mission & Strategic Plan
Mission Statement
Flathead Valley Community College promotes excellence in lifelong learning, focused on student success and community needs.
Strategic Plan
The strategic plan outlines how FVCC fulfills the mission of the college. The strategic plan sets priorities, focuses resources, and ensures the college is working toward common goals based on mission fulfillment.
The strategic plan aligns with the seven-year accreditation cycle and also reflects FVCC’s commitment to student success.
FVCC Strategic Plan 2020-2027
Goal 1: Increase Completion of Degrees and Certificates
Objective 1a: Offer streamlined pathways for students to graduate
Objective 1b: Expand learning opportunities and remove access barriers
Goal 2: Cultivate a Supportive Teaching and Learning Environment
Objective 2a: Provide, encourage, and support faculty and staff professional development opportunities
Objective 2b: Employ evidence-based college-wide practices to optimize student success and mission fulfillment
Goal 3: Foster an Environment
of Access, Belonging, and Success
Objective 3a: Instill an inclusive, welcoming environment that creates a sense of belonging for students, faculty, staff, and the community
Objective 3b: Increase opportunities to develop global citizenship and appreciation of diversity
Goal 4: Enhance Institutional Vitality
Objective 4a: Increase access to data
Objective 4b: Leverage data to inform decisions, resource allocation, and improvement initiatives
Objective 4c: Improve internal and external communication

site_pages.title: About the Flathead Valley
site_pages.full_text:
About the Flathead Valley
Tucked into the northwest corner of Montana, the Flathead Valley lies between Glacier National Park and Flathead Lake, the largest natural freshwater lake west of the Mississippi.
About Kalispell and the Flathead Valley
Kalispell is the largest town in the valley and the home of Flathead Valley Community College’s main Kalispell campus.
Kalispell is home to many local businesses, restaurants, and boutiques, in addition to chain restaurants and stores within walking distance of the college campus for convenience to those living in student housing.
Known as a mecca for the outdoors, Kalispell and surrounding communities of Whitefish, Columbia Falls, Bigfork, Lakeside, and Somers boast a vibrant community of active, passionate citizens.
Theater companies, live music, creative arts endeavors, museums, and cultural institutions abound in addition to recreational opportunities in northwest Montana’s four distinct seasons.
Recreation
Flathead Valley is the western gateway to Glacier National Park, one of the jewels in the crown of the national park system. The valley also lies adjacent to more than 1.7 million acres of federal wilderness in the form of the Great Bear - Bob Marshall Wilderness complex. Other nature highlights include the National Bison Range and the Jewel Basin Hiking Area.
Conditions on the rivers and lakes of the Flathead Valley range from placid water of Ashley Creek to raging whitewater. Among the popular whitewater runs are the Middle Fork of the Flathead River near West Glacier, and the North Fork of the Flathead north of Columbia Falls.
Winter sports include skiing, snowboarding, snowshoeing, cross country skiing, ice skating, hockey, and much more. Two ski areas in close proximity to the Kalispell campus include Whitefish Mountain Resort in Whitefish and Blacktail Mountain in Lakeside.
FVCC also includes the Lincoln County Campus, serving all of Lincoln County and located in Libby.
(Some information adapted from
gonorthwest.com
)

site_pages.title: Board of Trustees
site_pages.full_text:
Board of Trustees
The Flathead Valley Community College Board of Trustees is the governing body of the college. Trustees are charged with the primary responsibilities of establishing college policies and selecting a President to administer the operations of the institution.
View Policies and Procedures
The seven Trustees are elected by the citizens of Flathead County and serve three-year terms on a rotating basis, with elections held yearly on the first Tuesday following the first Monday in May.
Board Meeting Information
Meetings of the Flathead Valley Community College Board of Trustees occur monthly on Mondays at 3:45pm in the Boardroom, Blake Hall, FVCC Kalispell campus. The meeting schedule is below.
Meeting minutes will be posted following approval at the next board meeting.
There is no board meeting in August.

Board of Trustees
Term:
2015-Present
Email Callie Langohr
A Montana native, Callie Langohr was elected to the Flathead Valley Community College Board of Trustees in 2015. She is a retired Kalispell Public Schools Assistant Superintendent. Callie worked in the school district for twenty-nine years prior to her retirement in June 2022. During her tenure with the district Callie was a Flathead High School principal and served as the first principal of Glacier High School.
Callie holds a Bachelor of Science degree in Mathematics with a Physical Science minor from Montana State University. Her Master of Education in Career Guidance and Counseling is from Montana State University Northern. Prior to her public education service, Callie and her husband were owner/operators of a Montana McDonald’s franchise. She started her career with McDonald’s Corporation in 1976 and sold the franchise in 1993.
Term:
2022-Present
Email Travis Steindorf
Travis Steindorf joined the FVCC Board of Trustees in May of 2022. Travis is a 4th generation Montanan growing up in the Mission Valley. He graduated from the University of Montana with a Bachelor of Science in Business Administration. He is the owner of Glacier Appraisal and has been an appraiser in the valley since 2003. Additionally, he is a member of Daybreak Rotary and currently serves on the board. Travis, his wife Rachel, and their two kids enjoy hiking and exploring Montana as well as spending time on Flathead Lake.
Term:
2017-Present
Email Peter Akey
Pete Akey has served on the FVCC Board of Trustees since May 2017. Born and raised in Whitefish, Pete is a graduate of Whitefish High School and the University of Montana. As a member of the Montana Army National Guard since 1999, Pete has been deployed overseas several times. Pete is the Commercial Loan Department Manager for Glacier Bank and has served in various roles for the company over the last decade. Additionally, he serves on the board of directors of Big Brothers Big Sisters of Flathead County and the Montana Ambassadors. Pete, his wife and their three children live in Whitefish and enjoy all that Montana has to offer.
Term:
2016-Present
Email Kelly Dowling Stimpson, J.D.
Kelly Dowling Stimpson, J.D. has served on the FVCC Board of Trustees since January 2016. In her professional capacity, she has represented and provided legal advice to public school districts for more than 16 years. Prior to that, she had a general litigation practice with a focus on insurance defense, banking and securities fraud litigation. While practicing in Mississippi, Kelly coached the Tupelo High School tennis team and volleyball team to three state championships. Kelly matriculated at Stanford University and Brigham Young University and received her Juris Doctorate from the University of San Diego School of Law. Currently, she is Associate General Counsel of Logan Health. Kelly and her husband, Dr. Kim Stimpson, reside in Bigfork and have five children and many grandchildren. In her spare time, she enjoys outdoor activities, sports and baking.
Term:
2025-Present
Email Mark Ruby
Mark Ruby joined the FVCC Board of Trustees in 2025. A Kalispell native, Mark grew up riding his bike around the FVCC campus before attending Flathead High School and earning his degree from the University of Montana. He later completed his Emergency Medical Technician certificate through FVCC.
For the past 15 years, Mark has worked as a wildlife biologist with the U.S. Forest Service and now serves as manager of the wildlife program on the Flathead National Forest. He and his family continue to call Kalispell home and enjoy exploring the outdoors throughout the Flathead Valley.
Term:
2023-Present
Email Jamie Pickering
No biography provided.
Term:
2025-Present
Email Mary Sterhan
Mary Sterhan joined the FVCC Board of Trustees in October of 2025. She is a 4th generation resident of Flathead County and grew up on her family’s homestead near Columbia Falls. She graduated from Carroll College with degrees in political science and business-finance. She recently retired as CEO of Greater Valley Health Center after a long career in health care administration. Her son, daughter-in-law and most special granddaughter live In Helena. She is looking forward to spending time with family, traveling, catching up on her reading and serving on the FVCC Board.

site_pages.title: Faculty
site_pages.full_text:
Faculty
Faculty at FVCC are highly experienced in their fields and are continually establishing research projects, building partnerships, and writing academic articles to be published in national journals.
Faculty at FVCC create the rigorous academic programs and take pride in the academic opportunities students have at FVCC. Faculty work closely with students for an individualized learning experience that students can only find at our unique, tight-knit college.
Every month, faculty share highlights of their programs and academic work with the Board of Trustees which are also shared here.
Faculty Directory
Visit our Directory for additional details on our faculty and staff, including contact and biography information. You can also visit our
LCC Faculty
page.
Visit the Directory
Faculty Highlight
FVCC associate professors of chemistry Jessica Ennist, Ph.D. and David Long, Ph.D. created a course-based research experience for chemistry students at FVCC to make undergraduate research available to all enrolled general chemistry students.
“Students in chemistry courses will get to be a scientist on day one,” says Dr. Long. The research will involve water quality samples that can be put to use by the Montana Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ).
“Our FVCC students have had enormous success by participating in our faculty-led undergraduate research experiences,” explains Dr. Ennist.
“You don’t have to wait for upper-level courses to really dig into science–we want it to be hands on for all our students,” says Long.

site_pages.title: FVCC Foundation
site_pages.full_text:
FVCC Foundation
The FVCC Foundation supports scholarships, academic programs and learning facilities at Flathead Valley Community College.
Download the 2025 President’s Report
(PDF)
Buy Tickets Online
Bibler Tour Tickets Open April 15
Sponsorship Opportunities
Volunteer With Us
Invest in Students and our Community
$2M
scholarship dollars
687
students awarded scholarships
80%
of students need financial aid
Quote icon
Once I started at FVCC and  earned scholarships, I realized  that people were investing in me.  I really felt like I owed everyone  my best and to give school my all. I'm excited to see what comes next and find out ways that I can help.
Vanessa Palmer
Substance Abuse Counseling
Quote icon
I never knew I could be that good in chemistry until Dr. Ennist showed  me she believed that I could do it. And that was really important for me. I never thought I would have this love of chemistry if I had only pursued math.
Mbiya Dieu "Merci" Mpunga
Associate of Science, Honors, Honors Program
Quote icon
Thanks to scholarships, I was able to focus on my  studies and be a mom and be present for my family.  Thank you a million times to FVCC  donors, as they make a huge impact even if they don’t  see the results immediately.
Shiloh Widhalm
Nursing Alum, ER Nurse at Logan Health Kalispell
Ways to Give
Annual Fund
The Foundation accepts gifts of any amount for use in areas of greatest need at the college. Gifts received in this manner are used under the direction of the Foundation’s Board of Directors.
Annual Scholarships
Annually funded scholarships are funded each year by their unique FVCC donor. An annual gift of at least $500 may be used to provide a scholarship for deserving students within your specified criteria.
Capital Campaigns
To further the goals of the college and the needs of the community, the Foundation leads large-scale campaigns to develop new learning facilities on campus.
Employee Giving
Employees can deduct a set amount from their paycheck or make a one-time gift toward the Employee Giving Fund. These donations support student emergency needs and student scholarships.
Innovation Grants
As part of our mission to support programs at FVCC, the Foundation funds innovative projects up to $10,000 for faculty and staff that promise to improve the student experience.
Endowed Scholarships
Establishing a scholarship endowment can include contributions from family, friends, colleagues, a company, and others. Endowment funds are gifted with the express understanding that only the investment income from such funds may be used.

site_pages.title: Careers at FVCC
site_pages.full_text:
Careers at FVCC
Flathead Valley Community College is an incredible place to work. With a positive work culture, excellent benefits, and a lovely campus, we would love to have you join our team.
View Faculty & Staff Job Openings
View Student & Internal Job Openings
Employee Benefits
The average value of FVCC's
employee benefits
is $25,000 in addition to salary.
Health Benefits
Health benefits include a generous employer contribution toward the total cost, including options for vision, dental, life/ADD & long-term disability insurance.
Paid Time Off
You will receive up to 15 days of vacation leave, 12 days of sick leave and 12 paid holidays each year.
Retirement
The college participates in either the Montana Public or Teachers Retirement Systems for employees.
Employer Match
The college contributes an employer match up to 7% for participation in an optional retirement plan 403(b).
Tuition Waiver
Available for employee, spouse and up to two legal dependents.
Flexible Spending Accounts
Use pre-tax dollars for eligible out-of-pocket medical or child care expenses.
FVCC delivers measurable, long-lasting impact and tangible results for our community. What I most value about working here is our partnership-driven approach, focused on developing student-centered opportunities for a maximum return on investment.
Arthur Morken
Program Manager, Apprenticeship Work Based Learning
One of the best parts about working at FVCC is the effort everyone puts forth to support students. I feel empowered to do whatever I can to remove barriers, bolster student confidence and set strong academic and professional goals with students. FVCC is student-centered and I love that!
Dr. Eliza Thomas
Associate Professor, Education, Director of Early Childhood, Division Chair, Social Science
Living and Working in the Flathead Valley
The Flathead Valley provides a high quality of life by combining a growing economy with outdoor recreation. You’ll be minutes from Flathead Lake and close to Glacier National Park. Kalispell, Whitefish and Bigfork have walkable downtowns, numerous restaurants and 23 school districts, allowing you to balance a career with an active and family-friendly lifestyle.
City of Kalispell
Chamber of Commerce
Discover Kalispell
Flathead County School Districts
Flathead Valley Community College promotes excellence in lifelong learning, focused on student success and community needs. We are enriched by diverse backgrounds, perspectives and beliefs and committed to providing work and learning environments that educate about cultural differences. We will continuously promote and support a culture of respect. All members of our campus community share the responsibility of fostering a climate of mutual respect and caring.
View Current Job Openings

site_pages.title: Student Employment
site_pages.full_text:
Student Employment
Part-time work for students is an important component of the Flathead Valley Community College experience. The work student employees perform provides him/her with financial assistance and the opportunity to gain valuable work experience.
Below you will find useful information for all student employees at Flathead Valley Community College.
Careers at FVCC
A student employee is a part-time employee who must be currently enrolled at Flathead Valley Community College. Student employees are not eligible to receive benefits such as vacation pay or sick leave.
“Student employment” is an employee classification, like temporary, full- or part-time status.
“Work study” and “non-work study” indicates the funding source that pays the wages of student employees.
Students who would like to obtain part-time employment, follow these steps:
Find a job and apply at
FVCC Job Openings
Click a student job title to review the job description and minimum requirements
Click the Apply for this Position button located at the top of the posting, and then follow the steps to complete and submit the online application
Federal Work Study (FWS) is a federally subsidized program designed to promote part-time employment of students to help them avoid excessive debt while in college. By working, students gain experience, develop new skills, and earn money to meet their educational and living expenses.
FWS is awarded as part of a student’s financial aid package.
Awards are based on financial need and available funding.
Any changes in a student’s financial aid package may result in a decrease in FWS funding.
FWS Award does not guarantee on-campus employment.
Students must participate in the recruitment process by applying to current on-campus job opportunities and/or select College community service FWS off-campus tutor positions (America Reads).
FWS Eligibility
FWS is available to students who demonstrate financial need. To be considered for FWS employment eligibility, a student will need to complete and submit a Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA). Inquiries regarding FAFSA and your financial aid package should be directed to Financial Aid Office.
Students must be enrolled and maintain at least half-time enrollment (6 credits)
Audited classes are not considered degree-granting credits and do not count toward the credit hour requirement.
Students must maintain satisfactory academic progress as per FVCC college guidelines.
Student Employment Non-Work Study
Federal Work Study
Overview of Program
Part-time, on-campus employment opportunities for students regardless of financial need. Students do not need to fill out the FAFSA to apply for positions
Offered to students who demonstrate financial need as determined for Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA)
Financial Aid Requirements
Financial Aid Eligibility does not apply. No FAFSA is required to work
Students are awarded FWS as part of their Financial Aid package. Students must fill out the FAFSA annually and have financial need as determined by the Department of Education
Eligibility
Students must be enrolled as a current FVCC student
Students must be enrolled in at least 6 credits
Once you have completed onboarding, you will receive a “Welcome to Paycom” email from systemmessage@paycomonline.com with your Paycom login details. Upon initial login, you will need to set up your security questions.
You will be required to Punch In and Out at the beginning and end of each shift you work. Learn how to record time worked here:
–Time Entry Instructions–
*Student Employees are paid on a 2-week delay, e.g.:  Hours worked from the 1st – 15th will be paid on the last day of the month and hours worked from the 16th – 31st will be paid on the 15th of the following month.
Contact Us
For more information on student employment at Flathead Valley Community College, contact the Student Employment Coordinator:
Valerie Clement
Human Resources Partner, Recruitment

site_pages.title: Campus Safety & Compliance
site_pages.full_text:
Campus Safety & Compliance
Flathead Valley Community College is governed by a publicly elected Board of Trustees. Information about the Board of Trustees can be viewed in the
Leadership
section.
The FVCC Board of Trustees regularly reviews and updates college policy for accuracy, relevance, compliance, and legality.
In addition to Board policies, FVCC maintains appropriate policies, procedures, and regulatory compliance to ensure the college is a safe, welcoming campus for all students, visitors, and community members.
Appropriate information regarding conduct, ethics, and compliance can be viewed below. For questions, please contact the appropriate department.
Board Policies
Campus Safety
Title IX and Sexual Misconduct
FERPA
Student Consumer Information

site_pages.title: Campus Safety
site_pages.full_text:
Campus Safety
FVCC works diligently to provide safe learning environments for all students on both the Kalispell and Libby campuses. We strive to go beyond satisfying all the requirements outlined in the Montana Safety Culture Act enacted in 1993.
Call 911 if you believe a person poses an immediate danger to self/others, or there is an imminent threat.
Campus Resource Officer
Cory Clarke is a member of the Kalispell Police Department and works in conjunction with local law enforcement to provide a consistent security presence integrated with local law enforcement and policing systems. For campus concerns, disruptive behaviors or non-life-threatening emergencies, call (406) 407-1558.
Campus Resource Office
Student Center (SC) 152  |  Monday-Friday 8am-4pm
If you see something, say something.
Incident Report
CARE Referral
Title IX and Sexual Misconduct Report
Emergency Operations Plan
Campus Maps
Annual Security Report
Campus Hazing Transparency Report
Kalispell Police Department
Montana Sexual or Violent Offender Registery
Safety Measures on Campus
Flathead Valley Community College (FVCC) uses Regroup, a unified emergency notification system, to quickly alert the campus community in the event of an emergency. Alerts may be sent by text (SMS) to mobile phones, email, or voice calls. Notifications may include situations such as active threats, earthquakes, severe weather, or campus evacuations.
All students, faculty, and staff are automatically enrolled in FVCC’s Emergency Alert System through their campus email account. Students who have provided a mobile phone number to FVCC are also automatically enrolled to receive text alerts.
To add or update your contact information—including personal phone numbers or personal email addresses—or to manage your alert preferences, please visit the
Regroup Contact website
.
For added convenience, all users are encouraged to download the Regroup Mobile App to receive notifications directly on their devices:
Android:
Google Play Store
iOS:
Apple App Store
We strongly recommend that every student and employee log in to verify and keep their contact information up to date to ensure timely emergency notifications.
View Step-by-Step Instructions >
If it becomes necessary to evacuate your building for any reason, an announcement will be made to that effect via the campus emergency notification system.
Evacuation maps are posted throughout campus.
If it is not safe outside, do not leave your building until you are instructed via the emergency notification system or from your building coordinator.
Before entering stairwells or hallways, especially during a fire, check the door to see if it is hot or whether there is smoke. If either condition exists, use an alternate route.
If you are away from your office, follow the instructions given by the building coordinator, report to the designated area and check-in with the building coordinator in that area.
Do not re-enter the building until an “all clear” has been called.
You should stay at least 300 feet (length of a football field) away from affected buildings.
Kalispell Campus
rally point for evacuation:
Blake Hall, Business & Social Science
rally at the Arboretum (the tree area behind Blake Hall).
Student Center and Ross Hall
rally at Parking Lot B (the parking lot west of both SC and RH).
Founders Hall and Trades Institute
rally in the grassy area between the road and BSS parking lot.
Wachholz College Center and
Innovation and Entrepreneurship Center
rally at the tennis courts. Additional WCC rally point at soccer field.
Arts and Technology
rally at the soccer field.
Broussard Center
rally in the grassy area near Grandview Drive.
Libby Campus
rally point for evacuation:
Lincoln County Campus rally at the grass lot along the northwest corner of the LCC parking lot.
